title: Git Version Control
description: Git is the version control system that underpins all modern software development. Created by Linus Torvalds for Linux kernel development, Git is now used by every Enterprise company. Understanding Git deeply — not just add/commit/push — is essential for working in large engineering teams.

sections.title: Git Objects: Blobs, Trees, Commits
sections.description: Git is fundamentally a content-addressable filesystem with a VCS UI on top. Every file version is stored as a blob, directories as trees, and snapshots as commits — all referenced by their SHA-1 hash. Understanding this model is the key to mastering Git.
sections.codeBlock:
# Git's object model:
# blob  → file content (hash of content)
# tree  → directory listing (maps names → blobs/trees)
# commit → snapshot + metadata (tree hash, parent hash, author, message)

# Explore the object database:
git init myrepo && cd myrepo
echo "hello" > file.txt
git add file.txt
git commit -m "Initial commit"

# What just happened:
git cat-file -p HEAD
# tree <tree-hash>
# author Kallol ...
# committer Kallol ...
# Initial commit

git cat-file -p HEAD^{tree}
# 100644 blob <blob-hash>    file.txt

git cat-file -p <blob-hash>
# hello

# Key insight: Git stores content, not files.
# If you rename a file without changing content, the blob is reused.
# If two files have the same content, they share a blob.
# This is why git is space-efficient for text files.

sections.title: Branching & Merging Strategies
sections.description: Branches are lightweight pointers to commits. Creating a branch is nearly instant — just a 41-byte file. Enterprise teams use various branching strategies; understanding the tradeoffs between merge, rebase, and squash is an interview topic.
sections.codeBlock:
# Branches are just labels on commits
# main → C3 ← feature → C4
#              ↖         ↗
#               merge commit

git branch              # List local branches
git branch feature      # Create branch from HEAD
git checkout feature    # Switch to branch
git checkout -b feature # Create and switch (one command)
git switch feature      # Modern alternative to checkout

# === Merge ===
git checkout main
git merge feature       # Creates a merge commit
# Pros: preserves history, safe
# Cons: cluttered history with merge commits

# === Rebase ===
git checkout feature
git rebase main         # Replay feature commits on top of main
git checkout main
git merge feature       # Fast-forward merge
# Pros: linear history, clean
# Cons: rewrites history (never rebase shared branches!)

# === Squash ===
git merge --squash feature
git commit -m "Add feature"
# Pros: single commit per feature
# Cons: loses intermediate commits (debugging harder)

# Enterprise common workflows:
# Google: squash-merge (single commit per CL)
# Meta: rebase + merge (linear history)
# Amazon: merge commits (preserve full history)

sections.title: Interactive Rebase & History Rewriting
sections.description: Interactive rebase lets you rewrite history before sharing. Never rewrite history on shared/public branches. For personal feature branches, it keeps the commit log clean and reviewable.
sections.codeBlock:
# Interactive rebase — reorder, squash, edit, drop commits
git rebase -i HEAD~5     # Rebase last 5 commits

# In the editor, you'll see:
# pick abc1234 First commit
# pick def5678 Second commit
# pick hij9010 Third commit
#
# Commands:
# pick   = use commit as-is
# reword = change commit message
# edit   = stop to amend
# squash = combine with previous commit
# fixup  = squash but discard message
# drop   = delete commit

# Example: squash the last 3 commits into one:
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# Change 'pick' to 'squash' for commits 2 and 3

# Amend the last commit (fix message or add forgotten changes)
git add forgotten-file.txt
git commit --amend --no-edit   # Add to last commit without editing message

# Undo the last commit but keep changes staged
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Undo the last commit and unstage changes
git reset HEAD~1               # --mixed (default)

# Completely discard last commit and changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1        # DANGER: destructive!

sections.title: Git in Production: The Enterprise Workflow
sections.description: At Enterprise, Git workflows enforce code review, CI/CD, and deployment gates. Understanding these patterns helps you navigate large codebases and collaborate effectively.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Typical Enterprise Feature Workflow ===

# 1. Start from latest main
git checkout main
git pull --rebase          # Fetch + rebase local changes

# 2. Create feature branch
git checkout -b feat/my-feature

# 3. Make changes, commit often (incrementally)
git add -p                 # Interactive staging (hunk by hunk)
git commit -m "feat: add user auth"

# 4. Keep branch updated with main
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main     # Rebase instead of merge

# 5. Final cleanup before push
git rebase -i origin/main  # Squash fixup commits
git log --oneline          # Verify clean history

# 6. Push and create pull request
git push -u origin feat/my-feature
# (Open PR in GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket)

# 7. Address review feedback
# Make changes, then:
git add -p
git commit --amend --no-edit  # Keep single commit (if squashing)
# or: just add more commits (if merging)
git push --force-with-lease   # Safe force-push after rebase

# 8. Merge (via PR button — squash or merge commit)

# === Cherry-pick for hotfixes ===
git checkout release/v2.0
git cherry-pick abc1234    # Pick a specific commit from main

# === Bisect: find the commit that introduced a bug ===
git bisect start
git bisect bad             # Current commit is bad
git bisect good v1.0       # v1.0 is known good
# Git checks out a midpoint commit — test it
git bisect good            # Or: git bisect bad
# Repeat until the first bad commit is found
git bisect reset

sections.title: Git Configuration & Productivity Tips
sections.description: A well-configured Git setup saves hours. These are the configurations every Production engineers sets up on day one.
sections.codeBlock:
# Global configuration (set once)
git config --global user.name "Kallol Chakraborty"
git config --global user.email "kallol@example.com"
git config --global core.editor vim
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# Useful aliases
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all --decorate"
git config --global alias.last "log -1 HEAD"
git config --global alias.unstage "reset HEAD --"
git config --global alias.amend "commit --amend --no-edit"
git config --global alias.st "status -sb"

# Better diffing
git config --global diff.algorithm patience
git config --global diff.context 5

# .gitignore essentials for Linux projects:
# *.log
# *.swp
# .DS_Store
# build/
# dist/
# node_modules/
# .env

# === Production scenario: large monorepo ===
git clone --depth 1 <repo>          # Shallow clone (no history)
git clone --filter=blob:none <repo> # Partial clone (no blobs until needed)
git sparse-checkout set /path       # Checkout only specific directories

# === Stashing ===
git stash                    # Save uncommitted changes
git stash -u                 # Include untracked files
git stash pop                # Restore and remove stash
git stash list               # List all stashes
git stash drop stash@{0}     # Delete a stash

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Explain the difference between git merge and git rebase. When would you use each?'

A: 'Merge creates a merge commit that ties two branches together — it preserves history exactly as it happened. Rebase rewrites your feature branch commits on top of the target branch — it creates a linear history. Use merge for shared branches (main, release) to preserve the true sequence of events. Use rebase for personal feature branches to keep history clean before review.'

Q: 'You accidentally committed to the wrong branch. How do you move the commit to the correct branch?'

A: 'git log to find the commit hash. git checkout correct-branch. git cherry-pick . git checkout wrong-branch. git reset --hard HEAD~1. Or use git rebase --onto to move a range of commits.'

Q: 'A junior engineer force-pushed to main and rewrote history. How do you recover?'

A: 'git reflog on the remote (if you have access) to find the original commit. git reset --hard . git push --force-with-lease to restore. Enable branch protection on main to prevent this — main should require PRs and disallow force-push.'

  • Atlassian Git Tutorials — Visual explanations of Git concepts
  • Oh Shit, Git! — Practical recoveries for common Git mistakes

learningObjectives: Understand git Objects: Blobs, Trees, Commits
Understand git in Production: The Enterprise Workflow

analogy.title: Git as a Time Machine
analogy.description: Commits are snapshots in time. Branches are parallel timelines. Merge brings timelines together. Rebase rewrites history to make it linear. The working directory is your present. The index (staging) is your packing list for the next snapshot. Remote is a backup vault in another dimension.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Git Objects: Blobs, Trees, Commits
practiceLabs.task: Run `git` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man git` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man git`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: git operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Git Objects: Blobs, Trees, Commits?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Git Objects: Blobs, Trees, Commits is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: How do you list the contents of a git related directory?
options: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the git directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a git directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a git scenario, your monitoring alerts show git failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Git was created to solve a specific problem: 20,000+ developers contributing to the Linux kernel simultaneously
FAANG monorepo models (Google's Piper, Meta's Mercurial-based repo) are more sophisticated, but Git skills translate directly
productionTips: Monitor key metrics continuously; alert on anomalies before users notice.
Automate routine tasks; manual processes do not scale.
Document runbooks for common incidents; practice them regularly.
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, git version control is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
